channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. and Iran Trade Strikes, Further Threatening Negotiations
item.description: The U.S. military said Iran had launched a ballistic missile toward Kuwait. Iran’s Revolutionary Guards said it had targeted an unspecified U.S. base in retaliation for earlier strikes.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: The Mideast Is Baffled by Trump’s Call to Expand Abraham Accords
item.description: The president said more countries should be required to recognize Israel as part of a deal to end the war with Iran. Analysts say the chances of that happening are close to zero.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Isabel Kershner and Elian Peltier
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump with leaders from Israel, Bahrain and the United Arab Emirates at the signing of the Abraham Accords in Washington in 2020.

item.title: Oil Prices Climb on Renewed Hostilities in Middle East
item.description: Oil prices jumped after the U.S. said it shot down four Iranian attack drones, casting doubt once again on the prospects for a peace deal.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jonas Roosens/Belga, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Lebanese Resigned to a Long War, Even if U.S. and Iran Make a Deal
item.description: Few in Lebanon believe that diplomatic efforts to end the wider war will bring peace to their country, as clashes between Israel and Hezbollah intensify in the south.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A resident assesses the damage in the aftermath of an Israeli airstrike in Tyre, Lebanon, Tuesday.

item.title: Why Texas Students Are Being Tackled and Tasered, and Trump’s Latest Target for Retribution
item.description: Plus, inside the hunt for cheap gas.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Clare Amari and Corina Knoll
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Texas is home to nearly 400 school-district police departments, more than all other states combined.

item.title: Justice Dept. Is Said to Open Criminal Inquiry of E. Jean Carroll, Who Accused Trump of Rape
item.description: Ms. Carroll, who prevailed in a civil trial after accusing President Trump of sexual abuse, is the latest target in a Justice Department campaign going after his perceived enemies.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll leaving court in Manhattan in 2024. Donald J. Trump later lost an appeal of a $5 million verdict against him in the case.

item.title: These Are Some of the Trump Justice Dept.’s Targets
item.description: When he returned to office, President Trump vowed revenge against those he said had challenged or defied him. Here’s an incomplete list of the people his Justice Department has targeted.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Department of Justice in Washington, this year.

item.title: ‘Up for Grabs’: Can Democrats Sway Young Men Who Have Soured on Trump?
item.description: Many Gen Z men who voted for the president are dismayed by his time in office. But they are not hearing an appealing pitch from Democrats, either: “Both parties kind of get it wrong.”
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning and Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times

item.title: Jill Biden’s Reaction to Biden’s 2024 Debate: ‘He’s Having a Stroke’
item.description: “I had never, ever seen Joe like that,” the former first lady told CBS News. “Before or since.”
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.media:description: Jill Biden, the first lady, and President Biden after the presidential debate in June 2024. She understood he had performed poorly but told him that they had been counted out before.

item.title: James Talarico Opens Texas Senate Campaign Against Ken Paxton With Houston Rally
item.description: Personal attacks and a packed campaign rally set the tone for what is likely to be a rancorous contest for U.S. Senate between James Talarico and Ken Paxton in Texas.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
item.media:description: State Representative James Talarico campaigned in Houston on Wednesday.

item.title: Massie Visits Greene in Costa Rica for Fishing and ‘Spicy’ Political Talks
item.description: The two Republican critics of President Trump — Thomas Massie, who lost his House primary last week, and Marjorie Taylor Greene, who resigned from Congress — met up in the tropics.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

item.title: The Nonfiction Everyone Will Be Reading This Summer
item.description: Memoirs, histories, true crime, investigations and much more.

item.title: The Novels Everyone Will Be Reading This Summer
item.description: New fiction from Maggie O’Farrell, Ann Patchett, Colson Whitehead, Silvia Moreno-Garcia and much more.

item.title: Former Judges Urge Inquiry Into Deal Trump Struck With I.R.S.
item.description: The motion was particularly significant because it asked the judge overseeing the initial suit against the I.R.S. to examine the terms of the deal.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: In January, President Trump sued the I.R.S., along with two of his sons and the Trump family business, claiming they were owed at least $10 billion.

item.title: What is Trump’s $1.776 Billion ‘Anti-Weaponization’ Fund?
item.description: The Trump administration is creating a $1.8 billion fund to compensate people it says were wronged by the federal government, a group that could be largely made up of the president’s allies.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department is tapping an unlimited fund created by Congress to settle lawsuits filed against the government.

item.title: Guatemala Agrees to Joint Strikes With U.S. Against Drug Gangs
item.description: The deal is part of a broader effort by the Trump administration to press Latin American countries to agree to joint operations inside their borders.
item.dc:creator: Maria Abi-Habib and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:credit: Daniele Volpe for The New York Times
item.media:description: Security forces outside a prison in Guatemala City after a wave of riots and gang violence in January.

item.title: Court Orders Customs Chief to Address Compliance on Refunding Tariffs
item.description: The surprising demand to appear at a hearing suggested new concern about the Trump administration’s efforts to repay the full $166 billion owed from illegally imposed tariffs.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:description: Rodney S. Scott, the head of U.S. Customs and Border Protection, has been ordered to appear before the Court of International Trade next month.

item.title: ‘Vibrant as Ever’: Harvard’s Jewish Life Thrives as Trump Investigates
item.description: Trump officials have focused on episodes during protests over the war in Gaza at Harvard and other schools. Jews on campus describe a changed atmosphere.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: At Harvard University, students and others say the culture is different from the one depicted in the Trump administration’s lawsuit.

item.title: What Plunging Pork Prices Say About China’s Economy
item.description: A key measure of inflation in China, they hit a 16-year low, driven by anemic consumer spending and an oversupply of hogs.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:description: A pig farm in Taizhou, China. “Many hog farmers can no longer hold out,” Sun Haoyu, who raises hogs in Dalian, China.

item.title: Bare-Knuckle Tactics and Brash Promises: How Howard Lutnick Operates
item.description: Before becoming U.S. commerce secretary, Mr. Lutnick controlled 818 companies. A review of their dealings sheds light on his freewheeling first year in government.
item.dc:creator: Michael Rothfeld and Ana Swanson

item.title: 4 Takeaways From The Times’s Reporting on Howard Lutnick
item.description: The New York Times examined the dealings of 818 companies controlled by the U.S. commerce secretary, documenting a history of bare-knuckle tactics that extended into his time in government.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Michael Rothfeld
item.media:credit: Yuri Gripas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has boasted of using the power of the federal government to benefit taxpayers with aggressive deal making.

item.title: Online Scams Have Evolved in the A.I. Era. Here’s What to Do.
item.description: A criminal could be masquerading as a celebrity, web store or family member asking for your money. Detecting scams requires a new approach.
item.dc:creator: Brian X. Chen

item.title: Streaming TV Bundles Grow in Popularity
item.description: Companies and their customers are embracing bundled streaming packages — but for different reasons.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin

item.title: The Google Insider Trading Case Hits Polymarket
item.description: Charges against a Google employee who bet on Polymarket have raised concerns that the issue may dent momentum in the fast-growing sector.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: A new insider-trading case has rocked Polymarket, the popular prediction market platform.

item.title: In Argentina, U.S. Tech Billionaire Peter Thiel Finds An Escape
item.description: The billionaire’s new roots in Argentina are said to be partly motivated by concerns about the future of the United States and shared beliefs with Argentina’s right-wing leader.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Peter Thiel, right, arriving for a meeting with President Javier Milei at the Casa Rosada Presidential Palace in Buenos Aires in April.

item.title: Not Every Language Has Spelling Bees. Here’s Why English Is Perfect for Them.
item.description: English’s hybrid history, silent letters and overcomplicated rules are what make the competition fierce.
item.dc:creator: Nitsuh Abebe

item.title: How to Watch the Scripps National Spelling Bee
item.description: Nearly 250 contestants from around the nation and the world traveled to Washington for a chance at glory and a $50,000 cash prize.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.media:description: The winner of the Scripps National Spelling Bee receives a ceramic trophy known as the Scripps Cup.

item.title: Saints and Sinners, From the Vatican to the Alamo
item.description: Texas just got a lot more interesting.
item.dc:creator: Frank Bruni and Bret Stephens
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: What Cubans Really Think About Trump
item.description: A filmmaker asks residents of the embattled island what they would say to the U.S. president.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Seal

item.title: A Violent Education
item.description: We look at an extraordinary investigation into police officers in Texas schools.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: In Corpus Christi, Texas.

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Can Hardly Believe the Reality of L.A.’s Mayoral Race
item.description: “Mayor should not be your first job,” the host said of the former reality show star and mayoral candidate Spencer Pratt, who is polling in second place.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: Scientists Find a Potential Cure for Chronic Hepatitis B
item.description: Most people recover from the infection, but it poses great risks for those who don’t. A new drug may cure 1 in 5 of these patients.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Liz Hafalia/The San Francisco Chronicle, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A hepatitis B vaccine being prepared in 2009.

item.title: Why Pakistan and Afghanistan Are Still Fighting
item.description: Months after Pakistan declared “open war” on Afghanistan, neither side appears ready to back down, despite China’s efforts to mediate.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Zia ur-Rehman
item.media:credit: Tomas Munita for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Afghan Border Protection Forces officer, left, and Pakistani Army soldiers, right, at the border crossing between the two countries, in Spin Boldak, Afghanistan, in December.

item.title: Pressure Grows on Spain’s Leader After Police Raid His Party Office
item.description: After a judge accused his party of corruption, Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez of Spain has faced mounting calls to resign.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:description: Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez of Spain is dealing with a domestic crisis as he has sought to distance himself from a growing aura of corruption among his party and allies.

item.title: The Real Story Behind “Pressure,” the Film About the D-Day Weather Forecast
item.description: The new film “Pressure” tells the story of the fateful D-Day weather forecast. Here’s what it got right and wrong from the historical record.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
